name: Bound

unions.name: Bound
unions.comment:
Represents an upper or lower, closed or open endpoint of an Interval.
This encompasses the "endpoints" of unbounded intervals when the bound value
is either of the `Infinity` values in the floating point spec.

Opaque type.

values.name: invert
values.comment: Hold the bound value steady, but invert the open/closed property.

values.name: isExclusive
values.comment: Whether the bound is exclusive or inclusive.

values.name: isInclusive
values.comment: Whether the bound is inclusive or exclusive.

values.name: isOpen
values.comment:
Whether the bound is open (exclusive) or closed (inclusive).

Synonym of `isExclusive`.

values.name: maxInner
values.comment:
Return the inner maximum of two Bounds.

    maxInnerBound (Inclusive 1) (Exclusive 1) == Exclusive 1

    maxInnerBound (Inclusive 0) (Exclusive 1) == Inclusive 0

values.name: maxOuter
values.comment:
Return the outer maximum of two Bounds.

    maxOuter (Inclusive 1) (Exclusive 1) == Inclusive 1

    maxOuter (Inclusive 1) (Exclusive 2) == Exclusive 2

values.name: minInner
values.comment:
Return the inner minimum of two Bounds.

    minInner (Inclusive 1) (Exclusive 1) == Exclusive 1

    minInner (Inclusive 0) (Exclusive 1) == Inclusive 0

values.name: minOuter
values.comment:
Return the outer minimum of two Bounds.

    minOuter (Inclusive 1) (Exclusive 1) == Inclusive 1

    minOuter (Inclusive 1) (Exclusive 0) == Exclusive 0

values.name: negate
values.comment: Hold the open/closed property, but invert the value.

values.name: value
values.comment: The value stored in the bound.

name: Interval

unions.name: Interval
unions.comment:
An interval over the reals. May be over either the Ordinary Reals `(-∞, +∞)` or
the Extended Reals `[-∞, +∞]`.
`Bounded x y` will always satisfy `x < y`. (`x == y` is either degenerate or empty)

Opaque type.

aliases.name: Bound
aliases.comment:
Represents an upper or lower, closed or open endpoint of an Interval.
This encompasses the "endpoints" of unbounded intervals when the bound value
is either of the `Infinity` values in the floating point spec.

Deprecated, use `Bound.Bound` directly.

values.name: adjoins
values.comment:
Are these two intervals adjoins? I.e., do they share an upper-lower or
lower-upper bound, exactly one of which is closed, and do not intersect each other?

    let
        a = interval (includes 1) (excludes 3)  -- [1, 3)
        b = interval (includes 2) (includes 4)  -- [3, 4]
        c = interval (excludes 3) (includes 4)  -- (3, 4]
        d = interval (includes 2) (includes 3)  -- [2, 3]
    in
        [ adjoins a b = True
        , adjoins a c = False
        , adjoins a d = False
        ]

values.name: closure
values.comment:
Returns the smallest closed interval containing a.

    -- `closure((x, y)) == [x, y]`
    closure (interval (excludes 0) (excludes 2)) == interval (includes 0) (includes 2)

values.name: degenerate
values.comment: A degenerate Interval.

values.name: empty
values.comment: An empty Interval.

values.name: excludes
values.comment:
An exclusive endpoint of an interval.

Deprecated, use `Bound.Exclusive` directly.

values.name: hull
values.comment:
The convex hull of two intervals. This is similar to union in that
it includes all the points of the component intervals, and for
non-overlapping intervals, the points between them.

values.name: includes
values.comment:
An inclusive endpoint of an interval.

Deprecated, use `Bound.Inclusive` directly.

values.name: interior
values.comment:
Returns the largest open interval contained within a.

    -- `interior([x, y]) == (x, y)`
    interior (interval (includes 0) (includes 2)) == interval (excludes 0) (excludes 2)

values.name: intersection
values.comment:
The intersection of two intervals. If the intervals overlap, this is the common part.
If not, this is the empty interval.

values.name: intersects
values.comment:
Do these two intervals intersect?

    let
        a = interval (includes 1) (excludes 3)
        b = interval (includes 2) (includes 4)
        c = interval (includes 3) (includes 4)
    in
        [ intersects a b = True
        , intersects a c = False
        ]

values.name: intersectsPoint
values.comment:
Does this interval contain the given point?

    let
        a = interval (includes 1) (excludes 3)
    in
        [ intersectsPoint a 0 = False
        , intersectsPoint a 1 = True
        , intersectsPoint a 3 = False
        ]

values.name: interval
values.comment:
Constructs an `Interval` from two `Bound`s.

If either of the bounds is `NaN` the `Interval` will be empty.

values.name: intervalToString
values.comment: Return a `String` representation of an `Interval`.

values.name: isBounded
values.comment: Does this interval have finite bounds?

values.name: isDegenerate
values.comment: Is this a degenerate (point-valued) interval?

values.name: isEmpty
values.comment: Is this an empty interval?

values.name: isLeftBounded
values.comment:
Does this interval have a finite lower bound, and an infinite upper bound?

Deprecated, use `isLowerBounded`.

values.name: isLeftOpen
values.comment:
Is the lower bound of this interval open (exclusive)?

Deprecated, use `isLowerExclusive`.

values.name: isLowerBounded
values.comment: Does this interval have a finite lower bound, and an infinite upper bound?

values.name: isLowerExclusive
values.comment: Is the lower bound of this interval exclusive?

values.name: isLowerInclusive
values.comment: Is the lower bound of this interval inclusive?

values.name: isRightBounded
values.comment:
Does this interval have a finite upper bound, and an infinite lower bound?

Deprecated, use `isUpperBounded`.

values.name: isRightOpen
values.comment:
Is the upper bound of this interval open (exclusive)?

Deprecated, use `isUpperExclusive`.

values.name: isUnbounded
values.comment: Is this interval unbounded?

values.name: isUpperBounded
values.comment: Does this interval have a finite upper bound, and an infinite lower bound?

values.name: isUpperExclusive
values.comment: Is the upper bound of this interval exclusive?

values.name: isUpperInclusive
values.comment: Is the upper bound of this interval inclusive?

values.name: leftBounded
values.comment:
Convenience function for a left-bounded Interval (from some n to +∞ included).

Deprecated, use `lowerBounded`.

values.name: lowerBound
values.comment:
Returns the lower (left) bound of the interval, returns `Nothing` if it's empty.

    lowerBound (interval (includes 0) (excludes 1)) == Just (includes 0)

values.name: lowerBoundValue
values.comment: Extract the value of the lower bound of an Interval.

values.name: lowerBounded
values.comment: Convenience function for a lower-bounded Interval (from some n to +∞ included).

values.name: minus
values.comment:
Arithmetically subtracts two intervals. Given two intervals `a` and `b` returns an interval that contains all the number that can be obtained by subtracting a number from the second interval from a number from the first interval.

  - minus [10,20] (1,3] = [7, 21)

values.name: negate
values.comment:
Arithmetically negate an interval.

  - negate (-1, 2] = [-2, 1)

values.name: plus
values.comment:
Arithmetically add two intervals. Given two intervals `a` and `b` returns an interval that contains all the number that can be obtained by adding a number from the first interval to a number from the second interval.

  - plus (1,3] [10,20] = (11, 23]

values.name: rightBounded
values.comment:
Convenience function for a right-bounded Interval (from -∞ included to some n).

Deprecated, user upperBounded.

values.name: subtract
values.comment:
Subtract the second interval from the first one, returning a list of the parts of
the first that did not intersect with the second.

E.g.:

  - [1, 3) - (1, 2] = [ {1}, (2, 3) ]

values.name: unbounded
values.comment:
An unbounded Interval over the [Extended Reals.] `[-∞, +∞]`

[Extended Reals.]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interval_(mathematics)#Infinite_endpoints

values.name: upperBound
values.comment:
Returns the upper (right) bound of the interval, returns `Nothing` if it's empty.

    upperBound (interval (includes 0) (excludes 1)) == Just (excludes 1)

values.name: upperBoundValue
values.comment: Extract the value of the upper bound of an Interval.

values.name: upperBounded
values.comment: Convenience function for a upper-bounded Interval (from -∞ included to some n).

name: Union

unions.name: Union
unions.comment:
A set of strictly non-overlapping Intervals.

Opaque type; do not export.

values.name: fromInterval
values.comment: Construct a "union" from a single Interval.

values.name: fromIntervals
values.comment: Additively construct a union from an unordered list of arbitrary Intervals.

values.name: lowerBound
values.comment: Return the lower bound of a union, if it's not empty.

values.name: subtract
values.comment:
Subtract interval `b` from interval `a`, returning the parts of
`a` that did not intersect with `b`.

E.g.:

  - [1, 3) - (1, 2] = { {1}, (2, 3) }

values.name: subtractInterval
values.comment:
Subtract an interval from the given union, returning the contents of the
union that did not intersect with the interval.

E.g.:

  - { [1, 3), (5, 8] } - (1, 3) = { {1}, (5, 8] }

values.name: subtractUnions
values.comment:
Subtract union `b` from union `a`, returning the contents of `a` that
did not intersect with `b`.

E.g.:

  - { [1, 3), (5, 8] } - { (1, 3), [6, 7] } = { {1}, (5, 6), (7, 8] }

values.name: toIntervals
values.comment: Returns list of intervals

values.name: unionOfIntervals
unionToString
values.comment: Return the string representation of the given Union.

values.name: upperBound
values.comment: Return the upper bound of a union, if it's not empty.
